cards.title: Liver Detox — Phase I & II Pathways
cards._legacyHtml:
Phase I (Oxidation — CYP450 enzymes): Converts fat-soluble toxins → reactive intermediates. Sometimes MORE toxic than parent compound. Needs: B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, glutathione, antioxidants.
Inducers (speed up CYP450): Cruciferous vegetables, green tea, rosemary, Milk thistle (mild). Risky if Phase II is slow — accumulates reactive intermediates.
Inhibitors (slow CYP450): Grapefruit, alcohol (chronic), medications.
Phase II (Conjugation): Attaches water-soluble groups to Phase I products → safe excretion. Pathways: Glucuronidation, Sulphation, Glutathione conjugation, Methylation, Acetylation.
Phase II support nutrients: NAC (glutathione precursor, 600–1800 mg/day), Methionine, Glycine, Sulphur foods (cruciferous, garlic, eggs), Magnesium (methylation), B12 + Folate + B6 (methylation), Molybdenum (sulphation).
Key concept: Phase I and II must be balanced. Fast Phase I + slow Phase II = toxin accumulation.

cards.title: 7-Day Detox Programme
cards._legacyHtml:
Pre-detox (2 days before): Eliminate alcohol, caffeine, processed food, sugar, red meat. Hydrate 2.5–3 L/day. Begin lemon water (morning).
Day 1–2 (Elimination): Fruits and vegetables only. Freshly pressed juices. Warm lemon water 6 AM. Vegetable broth evening. Triphala 1 tsp at bedtime. Enema if constipated.
Day 3–5 (Monofast): Brown rice + steamed vegetables only OR fruit monofast. Milk thistle 140 mg TDS. Dandelion root tea 3 cups/day. Continue enema every other day. Walk 30 min morning.
Day 6 (Transition): Add legumes (moong dal). Include seeds (flaxseed, pumpkin). NAC 600 mg BD.
Day 7 (Reintroduction): Add eggs/fish (optional). Whole grains. Continue probiotics (L. acidophilus + B. longum).
Post-detox maintenance: Mediterranean diet pattern. 1 detox day/month. Ongoing: Milk thistle, NAC, probiotics.
Contraindications: Pregnancy, T1DM, CKD, cancer, eating disorders, children.

cards.title: Heavy Metal Detox
cards._legacyHtml:
Common toxic metals: Mercury (fish, amalgam), Lead (old paint, pipes), Arsenic (groundwater, rice), Cadmium (smoking, contaminated food), Aluminium (antacids, cookware).
Testing: Urine toxic metals (pre and post provocation), Hair mineral analysis, Blood heavy metals (for recent exposure).
Chelation agents (medical): DMSA (dimercaptosuccinic acid) — lead, mercury, arsenic. EDTA (IV) — lead, cadmium. Must be under physician supervision. Monitor kidneys and minerals during chelation.
Natural chelation support:
• Cilantro (coriander leaf) — mobilises heavy metals from CNS (use with chlorella)
• Chlorella 3–5 g/day — binds and excretes mercury, lead (especially from GI tract)
• Modified Citrus Pectin 5–15 g/day — urinary excretion of heavy metals
• Milk thistle — hepatoprotection during chelation
• N-Acetyl Cysteine (NAC) 600 mg BD — glutathione support
Warning: Natural chelation alone insufficient for high-level toxicity. Always test before and after.

cards.title: Detox Supplements — Evidence Summary
cards._legacyHtml:
Milk Thistle (Silymarin): 140 mg silymarin TDS. Hepatoprotective, antioxidant, anti-fibrotic. Evidence Level 2A for liver disease. Safe long-term. Drug interaction: may slow CYP3A4.
N-Acetyl Cysteine (NAC): 600–1800 mg/day. Precursor to glutathione (master antioxidant). Used in paracetamol overdose (standard care). Supports Phase II detox. Helps with PCOS, OCD, respiratory conditions.
Alpha Lipoic Acid (ALA): 300–600 mg/day. Recycles Vitamin C, E, glutathione. Heavy metal chelation (especially mercury). Neuroprotective. Caution: may lower blood sugar.
Dandelion Root: 2–8 g dried root or 4–8 ml tincture/day. Bile flow stimulant, diuretic, liver tonic. Safe. Caution in bile duct obstruction.
Glutathione (reduced): Liposomal form 250–500 mg/day. Direct antioxidant replenishment. Skin lightening effect (cosmetic use). Nebulised: lung conditions. IV: liver support in clinic.
Probiotics: Post-detox essential. L. acidophilus + B. longum + B. breve minimum 10 billion CFU/day.

cards.title: Detox — Contraindications & Safety
cards._legacyHtml:
Absolute contraindications for fasting-based detox:
• Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus
• Pregnancy and lactation
• Active eating disorder (anorexia, bulimia)
• Severe underweight (BMI <16)
• Chronic kidney disease Stage 3+
• Advanced liver cirrhosis
• Active malignancy (without oncology clearance)
• Children <18 years

Relative contraindications:
• T2DM on insulin or sulphonylureas (hypoglycaemia risk — requires medication adjustment)
• Cardiovascular disease (electrolyte monitoring required)
• Hypertension on medications

Detox reactions (Herxheimer-like): Headache, fatigue, skin breakouts, irritability — days 1–3. Reassure patient. Ensure hydration. If severe — reduce intensity of programme.
Red flags — stop detox: Syncope, severe chest pain, palpitations, blood sugar <60 mg/dL, vomiting >3x/day.
